repository_ssim: St. Catherine's Monastery of the Sinai
contents_tesim: The text falls broadly into three parts: 1) general rules on how to celebrate services. This section occupies the first seven folia of Quire one; 2) synaxarion, listing a selection of fixed daily celebrations, from the beginning of the liturgical year on 1 September until 31 August (from the second half of Quire one to the third to last folio of Quire five); 3) triodion, with the movable cycle, running from the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee to Easter Monday (the last two folia of Quire five until the end of Quire seven). The final part of the manuscript—from Easter Monday to All Saints’ Sunday (the first Sunday after Pentecost)—is missing. I have calculated that approximately two quires are now lost. All in all, this means that the Typikon originally consisted of approximately 70 folia (Rossetto)
Title of the manuscript (τυπικόν) on f. 71r
full_text_tesim: A single folio of Sin. gr. 966 corresponds to a single folio of the original manuscript, suggesting that the original manuscript was of similar dimensions: approximately 175x120 mm (oblong in height). The Schriftspiegel is approximately 135x90 mm, with margins measuring 20 mm (outer), 15 mm (inner), 30 mm (lower) and 20 mm (upper) (f. 71r)
Each side preserves between 19 and 26 lines of text, 6 mm apart and arranged in a single column

Minuscule with abbreviations and cursive elements. Yellow wash used for highlighting titles and rubrics (e.g. on fol. 53r), indicating south Italian origin. Typical Salentine traits are also: side bands that resemble vegetal motifs (e.g. fols. 55r, 81v, 93r), omicron en rondelle, initials with double outline, asterisk with arrowheads

Prickings are still visible in the margins (e.g. fol. 95), elongated in shape and likely made with a penknife. The scheme of ruled lines which support the writing corresponds to Leroy’s index X. This kind of ruling is often found in manuscripts written in Salento (Southern Italy)

The text falls broadly into three parts: 1) general rules on how to celebrate services. This section occupies the first seven folia of Quire one; 2) synaxarion, listing a selection of fixed daily celebrations, from the beginning of the liturgical year on 1 September until 31 August (from the second half of Quire one to the third to last folio of Quire five); 3) triodion, with the movable cycle, running from the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee to Easter Monday (the last two folia of Quire five until the end of Quire seven). The final part of the manuscript—from Easter Monday to All Saints’ Sunday (the first Sunday after Pentecost)—is missing. I have calculated that approximately two quires are now lost. All in all, this means that the Typikon originally consisted of approximately 70 folia (Rossetto)
The undertext runs parallel to the overtext
Title of the manuscript (τυπικόν) on f. 71r
